data.title: Tencent 1Q25: Growth Without Innovation, Betting On AI And E-Commerce Comebacks?
data.description:
Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) reported a beat on its 1Q25 revenue and largely in-line earnings. However, the quarter shares much of the same narrative as we have seen before.

On gaming, the ongoing reliance on existing titles that underscores a lack of game innovation, and, for new games, the IP and the storyline are largely based on proven prior IP, which once again illustrates the inability to create original content. Although the game saw solid growth for the quarter, much could be credited to a low-base effect and novelty effect of new titles, but this trend is unsustainable as trends on new titles remain soft.

Advertising revenue was largely driven by video accounts, mini programs, and search inventories as Tencent gradually incorporates AI upgrades into the ad platform that ultimately facilitate live streaming capabilities for content and merchants. Interestingly, Tencent disclosed that it has made ecommerce a standalone department, which implies that Tencent may be looking to aggressively scale up its ecommerce operation again after exiting the vertical in 2014.

In business and cloud, the decline in commercial payment provides a negative read-through on the consumption trend and a reminder of China’s ongoing weak macro backdrop.

While the US-listed OTC shares reacted positively to the print, the HK-listed shares sold off along with the broader market, as investors may be concerned about the possibility of Tencent ramping up on ecommerce investment in China’s maturing ecommerce market and the rising AI investment and whether Tencent could achieve positive differentiation amid a crowded field of AI applications.

While we believe the near-term easing of the Sino-US tension may help Tencent’s advertising and gaming business, we remain cautious on Tencent’s long-term outlook, as we would like to see more game IP differentiation and original content, and clear leadership in AI to become bullish on the stock.

Our thesis on Tencent is that the company can no longer rely on a volume-driven business model given China’s maturing internet market and needs to step up on innovation across its key business segments (see: Tencent: Betting On The Ever-Fading Classics). The company has yet to successfully pivot from its reliance on HoK and PUBG Mobile, two of which Tencent did not develop original IP. Tencent was only successful in monetizing this product in a large gaming community based on volume (i.e. Gamer base) rather than a quality-driven (i.e. IP-oriented) model, like that pursued by overseas game developers. Unless Tencent pursues a transformative M&A on the gaming side to address its innovation challenges, we expect revenue growth to be unsustainable (see: Ubisoft: Is Tencent Ready To Level Up?)

We remain bearish on Tencent and reiterate after the print. We believe that investors continue to gravitate towards Tencent given it is largely a consensus buy, but may be overlooking the execution risk and the potential geopolitical risk associated with the stock (see: Tencent: Geopolitical Risk Is No Game).

Games benefited from a low-base effect

Domestic games grew 24% y/y against a low-base quarter, driven by HoK and PUBG Mobile along with contributions from DnF Mobile and Delta Force. On international games, revenue saw 23% growth y/y driven by PUBG Mobile, Clash Royale and Brawl Wars. Management was particularly positive on the Delta Force, citing 12mn DAU in April and ranked 6th by average DAU in Q1 per data from QuestMobile.

For those investors who have played the original Delta Force, Tencent’s Delta Force deviates widely from the original gameplay and narrative. Tencent’s version took the Black Hawk Down movie narrative and added Call of Duty gameplay along with a freemium model to attract gamers.

Although the gameplay could be refreshing as it places the game under a modern graphic context, the level of innovation behind it is far from impressive, as Tencent once again failed to deliver original content. This aspect is our biggest skepticism on the company as it lacks IP creation capability compared to its peer NetEase or smaller peers such as MiHoYo.

Tencent recently took a $1.25bn stake in Ubisoft’s subsidiary which includes hit titles such as Assassin’s Creed. We are positive on this move as Ubisoft’s experience in Assassin’s Creed and Rainbow Six could allow Tencent to explore original IP creation, perhaps introducing content that could rival that from Tencent or Microsoft. Whether this will play out will be questionable, but we are disappointed by Tencent Games’ inability to innovate due to a lack of innovation elsewhere for them to mimic. This would be a challenge for Tencent as gamers become increasingly demanding of new IP, in our view.

Cautiously optimistic over the e-commerce strategy

Tencent’s management gave a very lengthy explanation of the thoughts behind its e-commerce strategy at the end of the call, which we find attractive and troubling.

On the attractive side, we feel that the Wechat ecosystem is heavily under monetized just by pure advertising or payments alone, and that ecommerce or any transaction-based interaction will be highly accretive to Wechat’s ecosystem and platform stickiness. Management avoided setting prescriptive KPIs, citing that ecommerce is a very long-term project with the near-term focus on improving the shopping experience through high-quality products and after-sales services, good pricing, sufficient inventory and merchants, and traffic referral to the merchants. To get there, live broadcasting and mini-shops will be critical in scaling Tencent’s e-commerce department.

On the troubling side, investors have lived through Tencent’s move into e-commerce, which failed and ultimately resulted in Tencent selling the platform to JD through a stake purchase. To be fair, a key reason behind Tencent’s prior ecommerce failure lied in its lack of a transaction platform (ie. Payment) and lack of scale vs. Alibaba, and Tencent has primarily addressed some of these shortcomings.

However, we are cautious in that China’s e-commerce platform remains very crowded between the conventional platforms such as Alibaba, PDD, and JD, and live-streaming players such as ByeDance and Kuaishou, all of which have higher scale and logistics advantage over Tencent.

We would be more bullish on Tencent’s e-commerce strategy if Tencent decides to create a cross-border e-commerce platform similar to that of Temu or Shein, given the lack of a compelling discount online retailer outside of China. However, since Tencent’s ecommerce strategy heavily relies on Wechat, which does not have a scale advantage outside of China, Tencent may be venturing into a cutthroat vertical that may not yield meaningful ROI. In other words, the idea of scaling e-commerce implies that Tencent lacks a visible growth strategy outside of China besides its acquired video game assets.

In conclusion, although Tencent delivered a better than expected Q1 result, we remain cautious on the stock due to lack of gaming innovation, China’s macro could negatively impact advertising revenue, unclear AI advantage and consumption concerns weighing on payment volume, and higher spending in AI and possibly ecommerce that result in limited ROI.

Within China internet, we prefer companies that have overseas exposure, hence our positive view on PDD (see: PDD Holdings: Navigating Domestic And Global Shifts For Sustained Growth) and Meituan (see: Meituan: On-Demand Momentum, Eyeing Lucrative Overseas Growth And Navigating AI Investments).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: Trading Platform eToro Impresses In 4 IPO Week
data.description:
Four IPOs and six SPACs debuted this week. Six IPOs and three SPACs submitted initial filings.

Global trading platform eToro Group (ETOR) priced its upsized US IPO above the range to raise $620 million at a $4.9 billion market cap. eToro’s platform aims to combine a social network with the ability to trade and invest, all within a regulated, digital investment platform for financial discourse. As of December 31, 2024, eToro had approximately 3.5 million Funded Accounts across its global footprint of 75 countries. The company’s total commission grew 45% in 2024, and its net contribution grew 41%. However, it is dependent on volatile trading volumes and prices, and is subject to varying regulations, especially within the crypto space. eToro finished the week up 23%.

Singapore-based digital asset-focused financing firm Antalpha Platform Holding (ANTA) priced its US IPO at the top of the range to raise $49 million at a $329 million market cap. Antalpha provides financing, technology, and risk management solutions to the digital asset industry. The company is the primary lending partner for Bitmain and provides supply chain financing solutions to institutional and corporate participants in the Bitcoin mining industry, offering loans secured by Bitcoin and Bitcoin mining machines. Its revenue jumped more than three times in 2024, although it demands on volatile crypto demand and prices. It finished the week down 6%.

Singapore-based oil and gas equipment maker OMS Energy Technologies (OMSE) priced its downsized US IPO at the midpoint to raise $33 million at a $382 million market cap. The company manufactures surface wellhead systems and oil country tubular goods, primarily for onshore and offshore E&P activities. OMS has a 10-year agreement with Saudi Aramco, which accounts for a majority of the company’s revenue. It finished the week down 13%.

Autonomous delivery systems developer Arrive AI (ARAI) completed its direct listing this week. Arrive AI is developing autonomous last mile mailboxes, designed to support deliveries done by robots and drones. Arrive AI is also developing a suite of software and services for its mailboxes, including features for scheduling, space optimization, and notifications. The company is in the development stage, and has generated no revenues to date. Its finished the week down 21%.

Six SPACs listed this week. Churchill Capital X (CCCXU), founded by former Citi executive Michael Klein, raised $360 million. Wen Acquisition (WENNU) raised $261 million to target fintech and blockchain. Cohen & Company-backed Columbus Circle Capital I (CCCMU) raised $220 million. Renatus Tactical Acquisition (RTACU), led by the former CEO of Digital World Acquisition, raised $210 million to target tech. Perimeter Acquisition I (PMTRU) raised $210 million to target defense and national security. Thayer Venture Acquisition II (TVAIU), the second blank check company formed by Thayer Ventures, raised $175 million to target travel and transportation.

Six IPOs submitted initial filings this week, led by mobile banking platform Chime Financial (CHYM), which filed to raise an estimated $1 billion. Space and defense company Voyager Technologies (VOYG) filed to raise $100 million. Japanese gaming platform provider CTW (CTW) filed to raise $21 million. Hong Kong-based traditional Chinese medicine clinic operator Charming Medical (MCTA) filed to raise $8 million at an $85 million market cap. Hong Kong-based consulting firm Zi Yun Dong Fang (YLY) filed to raise $8 million at an $83 million market cap. Hong Kong investor relations firm Thrive Capital (EFTY) filed to raise $7 million at a $100 million market cap.

Three SPACs also submitted initial filings. Jena Acquisition II (JENAU), led by SPAC veterans Bill Foley and Richard Massey, filed to raise $200 million. Blue Acquisition (BACCU) filed to raise $150 million to target manufacturing and data centers. Axiom Intelligence Acquisition I (AXINU) filed to raise $150 million to target European infrastructure.

Week Ahead

Two sizable IPOs are scheduled to price in the week ahead.

Digital health company Hinge Health (HNGE) plans to raise $410 million at a $2.9 billion market cap. Hinge leverages software to largely automate care for joint and muscle health. In addition to its tech platform, the company has also developed AI-powered motion tracking technology and a proprietary FDA-cleared wearable device. Hinge has delivered solid growth and improving margins, though it’s historically unprofitable with a $500+ million deficit.

Adtech MNTN (MNTN) plans to raise $176 million at a $1.3 billion market cap. MNTN's self-serve Performance TV (PTV) software platform allows marketers to combine the format of TV advertising with the capabilities of paid search and social advertising. The company has grown its customer base at a 73% CAGR since 2019 but operates in a highly competitive and rapidly evolving space.

Renaissance Capital's IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Kenvue (KVUE) and Arm Holdings (ARM). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 11.3% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 12.8%. data.title: Autodesk At $300: Premium Valuation Or Hidden Bargain?
data.description:
I analyzed Autodesk (NASDAQ:ADSK) over a year ago in March 2024:

  • Autodesk: Great Economics For A Long-Term Investment

In this analysis, I issued a ‘Buy’ rating for Autodesk for long-term investors. I highlighted characteristics such as the company’s robust profitability profile, rebounding free cash flow, and discounted valuation as factors driving my bullish thesis.

Specifically, I discussed factors that, I believe, make a great business and compared them to Autodesk. I said the following:

“If you asked me to name some fundamentals of a great company, I'd highlight a few key factors. First, a product that can be sold for many times what it costs to produce. Second, a product so good that customers not only remain loyal, but actually increase their spending each year. Third, a product that makes it expensive or difficult for customers to switch to an alternative. And finally, a company that doesn't cost a lot of money to run or maintain.

These key factors are fundamental reasons why I give Autodesk a 'Buy' rating. Namely, they sell a product for 10 times what it costs them to produce it, evident by gross margins of greater than 90%. Their product is so good that they could likely completely cut all new sales efforts and still grow up to 10% each year—made possible by net revenue retention between 100%-110%. Their customers have spent years and years learning their product, making it expensive and difficult to switch to an alternative. And finally, Autodesk only spends 2% of their free cash flow to keep their business running. That, to me, is a wonderful company.”

Since my rating, the stock has returned 13.2% compared to the S&P 500’s 13.9% return. Currently, Autodesk has a 24M Beta of 1.25. Using beta adjusted returns, Autodesk’s underperformance since my last rating has an even larger delta from the market.

Despite the slight underperformance over the last year, I remain bullish on Autodesk. Although I hold the same sentiment regarding my original thesis points, I wanted to refresh my coverage of the company. In this analysis, I will be discussing Autodesk’s recent financial performance, revisiting their valuation, and addressing their relevant risks. In the end, I will be reiterating my ‘Buy’ rating for the stock for long-term investors.

Recent Financial Performance

With Autodesk set to report Q1 earnings next week (5/22/2025), I wanted to recap their financial performance over the last year and highlight what to watch for in their upcoming earnings.

Over the course of the fiscal year ending in January 2025, Autodesk generated $6.1 billion of total revenue. For context, the prior year, Autodesk generated $5.5 billion of total revenue. This works out to year-over-year growth of 11.5%. Notably, year-over-year growth accelerated as it was 9.8% the year prior.

From their $6.1 billion of revenue, Autodesk generated $5.6 billion of gross profit. This implies a gross margin of 92%. Not only does this impressive gross margin indicate that Autodesk has fine-tuned the costs for the fulfillment of their products, but it also tells me that they have robust pricing power. While I believe that there is an array of factors contributing to their pricing power, the key takeaway is simply that they have it.

Autodesk also demonstrated sizable operating leverage over the last year. Revenue grew by 11.5% year-over-year, but operating income grew by 20.9%. On a more granular basis, SG&A expenses grew by 8.3% over the course of the year, and R&D grew by 8.4%. In all, the company produced an operating profit of $1.4 billion.

From this $1.4 billion of operating profit, Autodesk posted a $1.1 billion of net profit. This isn’t phantom profit either, as the company posted $1.6 billion of total cash generated from operations for FY 2025. It’s also worth noting that stock-based compensation moderated to $683 million during the year—down from $703 million the year prior.

With their operations generating well over a billion dollars of free cash flow each year, how does Autodesk’s management choose to allocate their excess capital? In the last year, the company allocated most of their resources into two initiatives: acquisitions and share buybacks.

Over the last year, Autodesk spent $825 million of cash on acquisitions. For more context, cash allocated toward acquisitions over the prior three years were $1.25 billion, $96 million, and $70 million. That sums to a total of over $2.2 billion over the course of those four years, or around $550 million per year on average.

Autodesk’s management has also been aggressive in buying back shares of the company. In fiscal year 2025, they bought back over $1.1 billion of shares. Over the course of the last four years, they spent more than $4.6 billion buying back shares.

In their latest earnings call, Autodesk's management noted that they plan to repurchase another $1.1 billion-$1.2 billion of shares during fiscal year 2026. To me, the fact that management is planning to continue aggressive buybacks is a clear vote of confidence. If they believed that shares were trading for fair value or even a premium, they would reallocate that capital to other uses. Some alternatives would be R&D or plowing it into more acquisitions. However, the fact that they are doubling down on buybacks signals that they see tangible opportunity given current share prices.

Despite the sizable outlays of capital used in these acquisitions and buybacks, Autodesk has maintained a well-capitalized balance sheet. They currently have $1.9 billion of total cash and short-term investments on their balance sheet. Their balance of total debt stands at $2.6 billion, however, they haven’t issued any new debt in the last three years. Moreover, with their Altman Z Score sitting at 4.73, Autodesk’s balance sheet appears strong.

In all, I’m relatively unsurprised by Autodesk’s financial performance over the last year. But, that’s the beauty of their business. They provide customers with incredibly high margin products that have high switching costs. As such, the durability and predictability of Autodesk’s revenue is among the best.

Valuation

In my last analysis on Autodesk, I discussed how their valuation wasn’t cheap, but it was fair. At the time, shares were trading for around $260 each. Today, they’re trading hands for close to $300. I maintain the sentiment that Autodesk’s shares aren’t cheap, but rightfully so. Let me explain.

Autodesk’s shares are currently trading at an EV/Revenue multiple of 10.3x, a GAAP PE ratio of 57.2x, and a Price/Cash Flow multiple of 39x. All of these are several times higher than the median multiples for the IT sector. The Seeking Alpha quant system rates Autodesk’s overall valuation as a ‘D-’.

This begs the question, does Autodesk merit the valuation premium? In my last analysis, I noted that comparing Autodesk to the broader IT sector isn’t exactly an apples-to-apples comparison, largely due to Autodesk’s superior unit economics. After all, the Seeking Alpha quant system gives Autodesk an ‘A+’ for their profitability rating. Moreover, if Autodesk's margins continue to expand, they could see some tangible lift in their valuation multiples, too. If this were the case, I don't think an EV/Revenue multiple in the range of 12x would be out of the picture.

One company that I believe stands as a very relevant comp for Autodesk is Procore Technologies (NYSE:PCOR). Procore provides software solutions for the construction management industry. Much like Autodesk, Procore has durable recurring revenue, high gross margins, and is exposed to many of the same macro-related risks.

So how do the two compare? To start, Autodesk’s business ($6.1 billion of revenue) is notably larger than Procore’s ($1.2 billion of revenue). This should merit Autodesk a bit of a valuation premium.

While growth is in the double digits for both companies, Procore outpaces that of Autodesk. Year-over-year revenue growth for Procore is 18.6%, compared to Autodesk’s 11.5%.

However, in terms of profitability, Autodesk is much more mature. Their gross margins are about 1000 basis points higher than Procore’s, and Procore is still yet to produce an operating profit.

With these characteristics in mind, I believe it would be reasonable for Autodesk to have a slight valuation premium due to their larger size relative to Procore. Moreover, I believe that Autodesk’s slower top-line growth is offset by their superior profitability profile.

So how do the valuations for the two companies stack up? Quite close, actually, with Autodesk commanding a slight premium in most categories. Autodesk’s EV/Revenue multiple of 10.3x is close to a couple turns higher than Procore’s 8.5x.

However, cash flow multiples make Procore appear a bit more expensive. Autodesk’s Price/Cash Flow multiple is 39x and Procore’s is 55.8x.

I analyzed Procore recently, and maintained a bullish thesis on the company. And considering that I believe Autodesk is trading for an attractive price relative to Procore, a bullish thesis for Autodesk follows suit, too.

Upcoming Earnings

When listening to past earnings calls, there are clear themes from the Wall Street analysts. With the maturing nature of Autodesk's software business, analyst questions generally revolve around three key things: investments that could boost top-line growth, the margin profile of the business, and macro-related pressures.

It's no mistake that these three factors drive the Q&A sections of the Autodesk earnings call. After all, they're the levers that drive the valuation for the company. For the company's Q1 earnings release, here's what I'd watch for regarding these three factors.

Growth Investments

The two things to pay attention to here will be the ongoing reallocation of resources within the company. Last quarter, Autodesk's CEO commented on the potential for investments in AI and the cloud to fuel future growth. He said the following:

We are reallocating internal resources to focus on the long-term. Autodesk is focused on the convergence of design and make in the cloud, enabled by platform, industry clouds, and AI. Our investments in cloud, platform, and AI are ahead of our peers and will drive growth by providing our customers with increasingly valuable and connected solutions and supporting a much broader customer and developer ecosystem. To maintain and extend this leadership, we’re shifting resources across the company to accelerate investments in these high-potential strategic priorities. We are also building the capabilities we will need to enable future optimization and ensuring that we distribute critical expertise globally to remain competitive, resilient, and flexible."

Is this just a classic example of executives spouting off buzzwords to excite analysts? Or will these investments have a material impact on the top and bottom lines? I'm hopeful the latter is true, but only time will tell. Getting a feel for management's sentiment regarding the progress of these growth investments will be crucial in their Q1 earnings call.

Margin Profile

In past earnings calls, Autodesk management has discussed high-level changes that they've made to their sales and marketing operations—in hopes of gaining greater efficiency there. They believe that this will drive noteworthy margin expansion in fiscal year 2026. As such, investors should closely monitor SG&A expenses as a percentage of revenue. It's by far their largest operating expense and hovered between 42% and 43% lately. If they do indeed see increased S&M efficiency, this number could trend down.

Risk of Macro-Related Pressures

The primary risks that I see for Autodesk are less related to Autodesk itself, and more related to their end customers. While servicing customers across industries including engineering, manufacturing, architecture, and construction certainly comes with its unique perks, it also exposes Autodesk to a host of macro-related risks.

In most cases, Autodesk’s customers are in capex heavy end markets. As such, their businesses are typically tied to interest rates and broader economic cycles. While I’d generally consider Autodesk’s products to be ‘mission-critical,’ it’s possible that economic turbulence could bode poorly for Autodesk’s customer base. Investors should pay particular attention to the sentiment of Autodesk’s management regarding macro-related headwinds in their upcoming Q1 earnings call.

Conclusion

Collectively, I maintain the same thesis regarding Autodesk that I did a year ago. They’ve built a strong business and have tremendous unit economics. Top line growth is steady, margins are expanding, and cash is being put to use either via M&A or share buybacks. And relative to peers like Procore, their valuation appears fair. As such, I am reiterating my ‘Buy’ rating for Autodesk for long-term investors.

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Nears Record Low As Inflation Expectations Climb
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Consumer sentiment fell for a fifth straight month as ongoing economic uncertainty and inflation worries continue to drag down consumer attitudes. The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index dropped to 50.8 in May, the second-lowest reading on record. This represents a 2.7% (1.4 points) decline from April and is lower than the 53.1 forecast. Furthermore, consumer sentiment has fallen by 26.5% (18.3 points) compared to a year ago.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regard to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month; a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment was essentially unchanged this month, inching down a scant 1.4 index points following four consecutive months of steep declines. Sentiment is now down almost 30% since January 2025. Slight increases in sentiment this month for independents were offset by a 7% decline among Republicans. While most index components were little changed, current assessments of personal finances sank nearly 10% on the basis of weakening incomes. Tariffs were spontaneously mentioned by nearly three-quarters of consumers, up from almost 60% in April; uncertainty over trade policy continues to dominate consumers’ thinking about the economy. Note that interviews for this release were conducted between April 22 and May 13, closing two days after the announcement of a pause on some tariffs on imports from China. Many survey measures showed some signs of improvement following the temporary reduction of China tariffs, but these initial upticks were too small to alter the overall picture – consumers continue to express somber views about the economy. The initial reaction so far echoes the very minor increase in sentiment seen after the April 9 partial pause on tariffs, despite which sentiment continued its downward trend.

See the chart below for a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We've highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession and included a callout to the most recent 12 months. At 50.8, the current level is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put today’s report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 39.9% below its average reading of 84.5 (arithmetic mean) and 39.0% below its geometric mean of 83.4, based on data dating back to 1978. The current index level is at the 0th percentile of the 569 monthly data points in this series.

This indicator is somewhat volatile, with an average monthly change of 3.1 points. The latest data shows a 1.4-point decrease from the previous month. To visualize the volatility, here’s a chart with monthly data and a three-month moving average. The bottom half of the chart includes real GDP, which allows us to evaluate the correlation between the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index and the broader economy.

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. To provide additional context, I’ve included a chart that shows the presidencies over our timeframe. As you can see, sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

In May, the CECI dropped to 57.6, the lowest reading since June 2022. This represents an 3.7% decline from the previous month and a 17.2% decline from a year ago. This reading was below the forecast of 59.6.

Meanwhile, the CEI fell to 46.5, the lowest reading since 1980. This represents a 1.7% decline from the prior month and a 32.4% drop from one year ago. This reading was below the forecast of 48.0.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations surged from 6.5% last month to 7.3% this month. This month’s rise was seen among Democrats and Republicans alike. Long-run inflation expectations lifted from 4.4% in April to 4.6% in May, reflecting a particularly large monthly jump among Republicans. The final release for May will reveal the extent to which the May 12 pause on some China tariffs leads consumers to update their expectations.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB business optimism index (monthly update here).

ETFs associated with sentiment include: Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Fundamental Weighting For Long-Term Outperformance And Enhanced Diversification: The S&P 500 Revenue-Weighted Index
data.description:
By Wenli Bill Hao

By weighting the constituents of the S&P 500® based on top-line revenue, the S&P 500 Revenue-Weighted Index offers an alternative to traditional benchmarks that are weighted by float market cap (FMC). This approach aims to better reflect the fundamental strength of each company, while offering better valuations and enhanced diversification. In this blog, we will analyze its methodology, examine short- and long-term performance, explore style tilts and assess diversification.

Year-to-Date Performance

So far this year, the S&P 500 Revenue-Weighted Index has outperformed The 500™ by more than 4.5% and surpassed the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index by more than 2.0% (see Exhibit 1).

Long-Term Performance

This recent outperformance is consistent with its historical track record. As shown in Exhibit 2, the S&P 500 Revenue-Weighted Index has outperformed both the S&P 500 and S&P 500 Equal Weight Index in terms of total return and risk-adjusted return over the long term, all while maintaining consistently lower volatility.

Upside Participation and Downside Protection

The historical capture ratios (Exhibit 2) show that the S&P 500 Revenue-Weighted Index has typically participated one-for-one in up markets,1 while delivering significant outperformance during down markets. This aligns with the design of revenue-weighted indices, which tend to provide a closer reflection of the broader economy and avoid overweighting overvalued stocks.

Methodology

The S&P 500 Revenue-Weighted Index assigns weights to its constituents proportional to their revenues from the past four quarters. To provide broader coverage and reduce concentration risk, individual constituent weights are capped at 5%, and the index rebalances quarterly in March, June, September and December.

Weighting by revenue may better reflect the broader economy, as revenue is directly tied to economic activity. Additionally, revenue serves as a direct indicator of a company’s ability to generate income and is less susceptible to accounting manipulations.

Valuations

As illustrated in Exhibits 3a and 3b, the S&P 500 Revenue-Weighted Index generally exhibited better valuations than the S&P 500 Equal Weighted Index and The 500.2

Sector Weights

As shown in Exhibit 4, revenue weighting has often resulted in more stable sector weights (as measured by lower standard deviation) compared to the weighting scheme driven by price, as revenues tend to be relatively stable. Moreover, the S&P 500 Revenue-Weighted Index held the largest weight in Consumer Discretionary, while Information Technology was the largest sector in The 500.

Diversification

When examining individual stocks, the S&P 500 Revenue-Weighted Index demonstrated greater diversification than The 500, featuring a lower max stock weight and a higher effective number of stocks (see Exhibit 5).

Conclusion

The S&P 500 Revenue-Weight Index presents an alternative to traditional market cap-weighted benchmarks, emphasizing fundamentally strong companies based on revenue. It has consistently delivered robust total returns and risk-adjusted outperformance over both the short and long term. In comparison to FMC-weighted benchmarks, it has historically offered greater value tilt, reduced concentration risk and more stable sector weights.

1 The market is defined as the monthly performance of the underlying benchmark (i.e., S&P 500) from Dec. 31, 1994, to March 31, 2025.

2 Historic price/book ratios show a similar trend to price/earnings and price/sales ratios.

3 Effective number of stocks is the inverse of the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI), which is the sum of squared stock weights for each index in each period.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit S&P Dow Jones Indices. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: A Tale Of Two Markets
data.description:
By Anu Ganti

After the volatility witnessed in early April, U.S. equities have staged a remarkable recovery over the past couple of weeks, with the S&P 500® up 1% YTD through May 15, 2025, reversing its 15% YTD decline through April 8, 2025. Thanks to optimism surrounding easing tariff tensions and strong Big Tech earnings, mega caps have rebounded, with the S&P 500 Top 50 outperforming The 500™ by 1.2% QTD.

The outperformance of larger stocks has been driven by the Information Technology (IT) sector, with the S&P 500 Information Technology up 14% QTD. Exhibit 1 illustrates that the disparity in performance between IT and the rest of the market has narrowed since the early April turmoil, as stocks from the sector have strengthened. The S&P 500 Information Technology has underperformed The 500 by 2% while the S&P 500 Ex-Information Technology has outperformed by 0.8% YTD.

Meanwhile, the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index, which has a smaller-cap bias by design, has underperformed The 500 by 3% so far this quarter. Exhibit 2 shows that the outperformance of mega caps and underperformance of equal weight has been a notable reversal compared to Q1 2025, which was characterized by mega-cap underperformance and equal weight’s outperformance.

Combining a size perspective with a sector perspective through a sector attribution of the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index versus the S&P 500 can help to understand the shifts in the equal weight index’s relative performance seen in Exhibit 2. Exhibit 3 illustrates that cross-sector weighting, or sector weight differences, were the key driver of the index’s outperformance in Q1 2025, stemming primarily from the underweight to IT. In contrast, so far this quarter, the underweight to IT has turned from a performance contributor to a performance detractor, consistent with the sector’s recent strong performance.

Although IT has made a significant comeback, the sector’s diversification properties have diminished. In Exhibit 4, we calculate the spread in trailing 12-month volatility between the S&P 500 versus the S&P 500 Ex-Information Technology. When this spread is positive, the inclusion of the sector increases volatility in the benchmark; when negative, the sector acts as a diversifier. Note that the positive spread for IT has continued to increase, approaching levels last seen during the Tech bubble.

Zooming out, Exhibit 5 indicates that dispersion among S&P 500 sectors has trended upward since the start of the year. With a 24% QTD performance spread between the leading Information Technology and laggard Health Care sectors, sector allocation decisions may continue to be especially important.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit S&P Dow Jones Indices. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - RBA, PBoC Decisions And U.K. Inflation In Focus
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Week in Review: Optimism Begins to Fade as Consumer Sentiment Deteriorates

Wall Street's main indexes were set to end the week on a high note, thanks to a temporary truce in the U.S.-China trade war. The S&P 500 was on track for its fifth straight daily gain, with all three major indexes showing weekly growth.

Stocks bounced back earlier in the week, with strong rallies on Monday and Tuesday after the U.S. and China agreed to pause their trade war for 90 days.

This pushed the S&P 500 back into positive territory for the year, the first time since late February. However, it’s still about 4% below its all-time high.

However, sentiment has suffered toward the backend of the week thanks in part to US data. Thursday's data was PPI, Retail Sales and the NFIB small business optimism index and these revealed some concerns which have weighed slightly on overall sentiment ahead of the weekend.

The biggest concern came from small businesses, who are getting more pessimistic about the economy.

The NFIB Small Business Optimism Index declined 1.6 points in April, to 95.8, its lowest since October 2024. 6 of the 10 index components decreased, with expected business conditions having the most negative contribution. Over the last 4 months, the index has fallen 9.3 points, the sharpest drop since the 2020 pandemic.

At the same time, the share of small firms expecting better business conditions 6 months from now has plummeted 37 percentage points, to 15%, the lowest since October 2024.

The mood remained sour after Friday's release of the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Preliminary Data, which showed the index dropped sharply to 50.8 in May 2025, down from 52.2 in April and much lower than the expected 53.4, based on early estimates. This is the fifth monthly drop in a row, the lowest since June 2022, and the second-lowest ever recorded.

Rising inflation worries and concerns about tariffs are hurting confidence. Both the current conditions index (57.6 vs 59.8) and future expectations (46.5 vs 47.3) got worse. Personal finances took a big hit, falling nearly 10% due to weaker incomes. Nearly 75% of consumers mentioned tariffs as a concern, up from 60% in April, showing trade policy uncertainty is a major worry.

Inflation expectations for the next year jumped to 7.3%, the highest since 1981, up from 6.5%, while long-term inflation expectations also rose slightly to 4.6% from 4.4%.

The consumer sentiment data has definitely dampened the mood heading into the weekend. However, the mood remains optimistic for now, but if data in the week and weeks ahead continue to deteriorate, even trade deals may not be enough to lift optimism around a potential global slowdown.

For a more in-depth outlook on Gold and Crypto, the following articles may be worth a read:

  • Crypto Market Today: Bitcoin (BTC/USD) Consolidation and Ripple (XRP/USD) Potential Breakout
  • Gold (XAU/USD) Surges Beyond $3200/oz on Weak US Data, DXY Slips

The Week Ahead: Central Banks in Asia Pacific and UK Inflation on Deck

The week ahead has several important data releases lined up. The US gets a bit of a data break in the week ahead with Europe and the UK taking center stage. We will also have a busy week in the Asia Pacific region where the data dump for China begins

Asia Pacific Markets Outlook

The Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) is expected to lower its cash rate by 0.25% to 3.85%. While April's inflation numbers were higher than expected, core inflation, which the RBA focuses on, dropped to 2.9% year-on-year. This is the first time since 2021 that core inflation is within the RBA's target range.

China's April economic data kicks off on Monday. Retail sales are expected to grow to 6.3% from 5.9% last year, showing stronger domestic demand. Fixed-asset investment should stay steady, rising slightly to 4.3%. Industrial production may slow to 6.0% from 7.7%, as earlier PMI and trade data hinted at a mild slowdown. Property prices in 70 cities, also out Monday, will reveal if the market has hit bottom, with some cities stabilizing or seeing slight increases, though overall prices remain negative. On Tuesday, banks are likely to cut 1-year and 5-year loan rates by 0.10%, following a recent rate cut by the central bank.

Japan's data is expected to weaken, as US tariffs impact exports and manufacturing. Flash manufacturing PMI is likely to drop. Exports should grow by 2.5% in April compared to last year, but slower than March. Strong exports to Asia will help offset the drop in US exports. A big fall in imports should keep the trade surplus intact. Meanwhile, core inflation is expected to rise sharply in April, driven mainly by higher service prices.

Europe, US and UK Market Outlook

With 30-year mortgage rates stuck just below 7% and the average home loan at $450,000, monthly payments are nearly $3,000. This highlights how unaffordable the US housing market has become, leading to very low mortgage applications. Recent financial market volatility has made buyers even more hesitant, which explains why home builders are feeling so down. This is reflected in the sharp drop in the NAHB sentiment index in May. All of this suggests that sales of new and existing homes will remain very slow in the next batch of data.

In the Euro Area, the economy hasn’t shown major problems from the trade war. April’s manufacturing data was surprisingly strong, likely boosted by businesses rushing to act before US tariffs hit. May’s data will reveal if the sector can handle global challenges. I am not too optimistic, but I do admit, I am a fan of surprises.

Consumer confidence will be key following the revelations from US consumer confidence data this past week. Consumer confidence has dropped a lot recently, meaning people are less likely to spend the extra money they’re earning from higher wages. However, if people start worrying less about the global economy, we might see stronger spending at home.

We also have the EU-UK summit which kicks off on Monday is mainly about agreeing on a defence partnership, but the UK also wants to make progress on economic issues. The UK is pushing for an agreement on food standards to reduce border checks. However, disagreements over migration and fishing are making it harder to improve economic ties.

April’s inflation data is important because many service prices go up annually during this time. In the past, this data has often been higher than expected. However, we think services inflation will be lower than the Bank of England’s 5% forecast. If we’re right, it might not lead to a rate cut in June, but it could make one more likely in August.

Chart of the Week - US Dollar Index (DXY)

This week's focus remains on the US Dollar Index.

The index has struggled to build on Monday's impressive gains following the US - China 90-day pause.

US data did little to assist the greenback this week as it toiled around resistance at 101.18 since Wednesday.

The index is on course to close the week with marginal gains, and the trendline breakout still supports the idea of a move higher.

This is coupled with the period-14 RSI holding above the 50-neutral level, which is seen as a sign of bullish momentum still being in play.

The lack of data next week means the DXY may need another catalyst if the bulls are to take control. There have been rumors of potential trade deal announcements coming soon.

This could be the push the index needs right now for bulls to really make a serious push to the upside.

US Dollar Index (DXY) Daily Chart - May 16, 2025

Key Levels to Consider:

Support

  • 100.61
  • 100.00
  • 100.00

Resistance

  • 101.18
  • 102.16
  • 102.64

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ABN AMRO: All Eyes On November
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2025, ABN AMRO (OTCPK:AAVMY) has outperformed the iShares MSCI Europe Financials ETF (EUFN), delivering a return of ~60%:

I previously covered ABN AMRO with a Buy rating back in February 2025, arguing the shares presented a buying opportunity thanks to their very undemanding valuation which compensated for risks related to ongoing ECB rate cuts.

In this article, I will share my highlights from the bank's Q1 2025 results and discuss the potential for a higher profitability target when the bank presents its new strategy in November 2025. I confirm my Buy rating on the shares, as I reckon ABN AMRO will increase its return on equity target to 11-12% by 2028 (which admittedly would still put it below most peers).

Company Overview

You can access all ABN AMRO results on its website here. ABN AMRO reports results in three key business segments, Personal & Business Banking at 44% of Q1 2025 operating income, Wealth Management at 18%, and Corporate Banking at 38%, with the remaining results booked in Group Functions.

Net interest income /NII/ accounted for 72% of Q1 2025 operating income, while net fee and commission income contributed 24%. ABN AMRO's high NII reliance is a key concern amid ongoing ECB rate cuts.

Q1 2025 Results Overview

Personal & Business Banking delivered 3% lower Y/Y operating income in Q1 2025 (driven by lower NII), while operating expenses only dropped 2%. Even so, the segment's net profit for the period was flat Y/Y thanks to provision release in loan impairments.

Wealth Management also saw a 2% Y/Y decrease in operating income. Expenses increased by 11% Y/Y, partly driven by the acquisition of neobroker BUX. As a result, the segment's net profit slumped 21% Y/Y.

Corporate Banking suffered the steepest drop in operating income, down 7% Y/Y (driven by lower NII and other income). At the same time, expenses increased 4% Y/Y, which, together with a normalizing cost of risk, resulted in a 24% Y/Y decrease in segment net profit.

On a consolidated basis, ABN AMRO delivered 2% lower operating income, 4% higher operating expenses, ultimately resulting in an 8% Y/Y decrease in net income. EPS stood at €0.69/share, while the return on equity was 9.9%, down 1.7% Y/Y:

I estimate tangible book value stood at €27.66/share, up 3% Q/Q. The bank's CET1 ratio was 14.7%, up 0.2% Q/Q, well above ABN AMRO's 11.3% regulatory requirement.

Overall, I think that the bank's results were somewhat disappointing across segments, mitigated by a benign cost of risk (just 1 basis point of loans) and an improvement in the CET1 ratio.

2025-2026 Outlook

Looking ahead to the remainder of 2025, we see that NII is set to moderate further to €6.2-6.4 billion for the full year, while the Q1 2025 costs run-rate will remain throughout the year, reaching €5.3-5.4 billion:

ABN AMRO's acquisition of Hauck Aufhäuser Lampe /HAL/ is set to close in the second quarter of 2025, impacting the CET1 ratio by 0.45%. Even so, we see that ABN AMRO is likely to have some spare capital for a share buyback later this year as the CET1 ratio remains well above the 13.5% target for 2026.

As a reminder, a smaller capital buffer will allow ABN AMRO to reach its 9-10% return on equity target in 2026 amid an expected normalization in the cost of risk and ongoing pressure on NII from ECB rate cuts.

Opportunity to cut costs

Even after the strong performance so far in 2025, ABN AMRO trades at a ~21% discount to its tangible book value, one of the highest discounts among European lenders with a high reliance on NII. This is largely explained by the bank's return on equity target of just 9-10% for next year, below most peers.

To address this, new CEO Marguerite Bérard noted:

...my priority will be to lead a strategic review of our activities, while building upon our solid foundations and strong market positions. We will focus on enhancing our profitability, optimising our capital position, right-sizing our cost base and achieving meaningful growth. The outcome of this review will be presented at a Capital Markets Day in November this year.

As such, I think it is reasonable to believe that ABN AMRO will announce a higher profitability target in November 2025. One area I see room for improvement is ABN AMRO's cost/income ratio, which is forecast at 61% in 2025 and 60% in 2026. For reference, ING Groep (ING) targets a cost/income ratio of 52-54% in 2027, while Commerzbank (OTCPK:CRZBF) targets a cost/income ratio of 50% in 2028.

I estimate that every 1% improvement in the cost/income ratio may boost return on equity by circa 0.3%, and hence, it is not unreasonable to believe that ABN AMRO will announce a marginally higher return on equity target, perhaps 11-12% by 2028.

Valuation

From the discussion so far, we can reasonably assume that ABN AMRO's 2025-2026 earnings multiple of 8x (assuming a return on equity of about 9.5% each year) turns to a 6.6x earnings multiple for 2028 as long as the bank does announce a higher profitability target (ROE of 11-12%) at its Capital Markets Day in November 2025. For comparison, financial stocks held in EUFN trade at an 11.6x earnings multiple.

Hence, even after the strong performance so far in 2025, I still believe ABN AMRO is worth a Buy rating, largely driven by my expectations for a focus on operational efficiency when the bank addresses investors this fall.

Of course, ABN AMRO's high cost/income ratio also presents an opportunity for other banks to acquire the small Dutch lender. So far, though, we have no indication of other banks expressing interest in acquiring ABN AMRO.

Risks

The main risk in the investment case remains ABN AMRO's excessive reliance on net interest income, which will only partially be addressed by the HAL acquisition. M3 money supply growth remains muted in the Eurozone, at only 3.6% Y/Y in March, below levels observed in 2015-2019 when interest rates were at much lower levels. As such, I expect the ECB to continue with rate cuts until lending growth picks up. This, in turn, will impact ABN AMRO's NII.

The second risk to mention is that the bank may not announce a higher profitability target this November, which will likely come as a disappointment for investors. Last but not least, the relative outperformance of the Dutch economy may reverse, impacting ABN AMRO's credit provisions, which have been very low in recent years:

Conclusion

ABN AMRO delivered poor segment operating performance in Q1 2025, with the impact on profitability mitigated by a benign credit environment. The bank's capital position also strengthened quarter-over-quarter, allowing ABN AMRO to absorb the HAL acquisition and potentially execute additional share repurchases in the second half of 2025.

ABN AMRO's competitors have announced ambitious cost/income targets for the next few years. As such, I expect the Dutch lender to focus on cost-cutting and operational efficiency on its capital markets day in November 2025.

I believe ABN AMRO will announce a profitability target of 11-12%, implying the company's current 8x earnings multiple is likely to shrink in the years ahead. As such, even after the strong returns so far in 2025, I still reckon ABN AMRO is worth a Buy rating.

Investors should consider the risks related to ongoing ECB rate cuts, no meaningful increase in profitability targets announced in November, as well as a deterioration in the strong Dutch economic momentum of recent years.

Thank you for reading.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ABN AMRO: Asset Quality Continues To Support Earnings
data.description:
Driven by ongoing low credit charges, Dutch lender ABN AMRO (OTCPK:AAVMY) (OTCPK:ABMRF) delivered a modest bottom-line beat for the first quarter, with attributable net income of €568 million landing ahead of company-compiled consensus. Provision-fueled beats often don't win much kudos from the market, but with a stronger-than-expected capital ratio strengthening the case for buybacks, ABN is currently up around 7% on its pre-results closing price, lifting it to its highest level since 2019. As for the ADSs, they've received an extra boost from a weaker U.S. dollar. That's helped drive an exceptional 57% return so far in 2025, including a roughly 33% return since my last update in February.

Back then, ABN was trading for just ~0.65x tangible book value ('TBV') despite earning a ~10% return on tangible equity. This pointed to a price/earnings multiple of less than 7x and, based on its usual 50% payout ratio, a high single-digit dividend yield. Fresh results provide a nice opportunity to revisit ABN's value case. Although its shares have re-rated, I remain bullish.

Asset Quality Still Padding Results

ABN AMRO has started its fiscal 2025 in seemingly good form, with first quarter attributable net income of €568 million (€0.69 per share) beating consensus by around €30 million, or 6%. This mapped to an annualized return on equity of 9.9% (similar based on tangible equity), in line with management's 9%-10% medium-term goal, putting ABN on course to meet initial 2025 consensus EPS of €2.39.

While the bottom-line beat was driven by lower than expected credit charges (more on that below), ABN's pre-provision performance looked reasonable enough, and management kept full-year guidance for the main drivers (net interest income and operating costs) in place.

Starting with revenue, net interest income accounts for over 70% of ABN's top line. Although it is a relatively simple bank, this line can still be somewhat complicated to get a handle on, with dynamics like its deposit hedges, mix shift, asset margins, volumes et al. all playing a role.

Broadly speaking, the first quarter saw weaker margins offset by better-than-expected volumes. Starting with the former, ABN's net interest margin fell 8bps year-on-year to 1.54%, missing consensus by the same amount. Lower interest rates are weakening deposit margins (the spread between swap rates and deposit costs) while asset margins are also a headwind, specifically in mortgages. Offsetting this, both loans and deposits grew year-on-year and came in higher than expected. All said, ABN generated €1.56 billion in first quarter NII, flat year-on-year and virtually in line with consensus.

Fee income was a little stronger, rising 8% year-on-year to €507 million and beating by around 2%. In its retail business, a pick-up in the Dutch mortgage market was good for higher advisory fees, while its corporate wing saw fees rise due to higher transaction volumes in its clearing business. ABN should close on its purchase of fee-heavy German private bank Hauck Aufhäuser Lampe in Q2, bolstering total fee income in the remaining quarters.

Turning to costs, operating costs were €1.31 billion. This was virtually in line with consensus, leaving the bank on track to meet guidance of €5.3-€5.4 billion for the full year. As for credit, I thought this could be a source of upside last time, and this looks like it is playing out, with ABN booking just €5 million in credit costs last quarter. This beat by over €60 million and equated to a cost of risk of less than 1bp. Higher residential real estate prices are credit positive for ABN, and with Dutch house prices up over 10% year-on-year, lower loan-to-value ratios ('LTV') contributed to a release in its retail business, offsetting higher charges on the corporate side.

Clearly, this line is padding the bank's profitability right now. If credit charges had landed in line with management's through-the-cycle target (15-20bps), ABN's annualized ROE would have been around 140bps lower. While that is something to bear in mind going forward, I would note that asset quality remains excellent, with various metrics (Stage 2 and 3 Ratios, percentage of residential mortgages in arrears, Forbearance Ratio, loans past due, etc.) either stable or improving quarter-on-quarter. This can help keep credit costs contained in the near term.

For readers worried about the possible impact of tariffs, I would think that ABN is probably one of the least exposed European banks to trade disruption, given over 60% of its loans are in Dutch residential mortgages.

Per management, losses on mortgages are mainly due to a combination of rising unemployment (which leads to an increase in defaults) and falling house prices (resulting in negative equity and a loss on disposal). In terms of the former, unemployment in the Netherlands remains near its recent historical lows, as shown in the chart above. On the latter, ABN's weighted average LTV is around 53%, with only ~2% of mortgages above 100%, as shown below.

Capital Surprises To The Upside

While credit-driven beats are often considered lower quality by the market, ABN shares are currently around 7% higher than their pre-results closing price. One possible explanation for that is the strength of ABN's balance sheet, with the bank reporting a Common Equity Tier 1 capital ratio of 14.7% at the end of last quarter, 50bps above consensus.

Why is a beat here noteworthy? Well, having historically delivered buyback updates at the beginning of the year, ABN delayed this year's plan to post-2Q25 results, disappointing the market. I did think this was a timing issue and nothing else, but anyway, a beat on capital levels bolsters the case for buybacks when it does finalize its offering. That's because management targets a 13.5% CET1, which implies around €1.7 billion in surplus capital - equal to roughly 10% of its current market value. Since ABN is primarily a yield stock (dividends and buybacks will drive most of its returns from here), the market's positive reaction to results makes sense.

Shares Still Look Reasonably Valued

ABN shares trade for €21.17 as I type ($23.67 per ADS), up around 25% from my last update (the ADSs have done better due to the weaker dollar). Naturally, that makes the stock a little less cheap than it was; relative to prior-quarter TBV, ABN has re-rated from 0.63x to 0.77x, while its price/earnings multiple has expanded from 7.1x to 8.5x (based on 2025 consensus EPS).

I think the shares remain reasonably valued in spite of this. In terms of earnings, consensus hasn't moved all that much. Analysts still expect ABN to make around €2 billion in annual attributable net income between 2025 and 2027, and this would keep the forward P/E in the single-digit area.

In turn, the above should map to a pretty attractive shareholder yield. Since ABN's organic growth potential is limited, it needs to maintain a relatively high total payout ratio in order to avoid capital accumulation. ABN's dividend is the main tool for achieving this, with management aiming to pay out 50% of net income this way. This implies a forward yield of ~6%. I think ABN could manage a further €500 million per annum in buybacks out of earnings, and that would take its total shareholder yield to around 9%. Given ABN's stronger-than-expected CET1, there's scope for additional capital returns as well, pushing the total yield over 10%.

More broadly, I think a mature bank earning a 9%-10% ROE should really trade for around 0.9x-1.0x book value, and this would still leave ABN undervalued by roughly 20%-30%.

Conclusion

ABN AMRO is off to a sound start to 2025, with first quarter earnings topping sell-side expectations. While provision-fueled beats don't usually elicit the kind of positive response that ABN saw post-results, a stronger than expected regulatory capital ratio augurs well for next quarter's update on buybacks. As for the investment case, while these shares have re-rated since my last update, a single-digit multiple of forward earnings continues to look cheap. Accordingly, I remain bullish.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
